policy_statement: Implement capacity building actions for technicians and decision-makers in climate vulnerability assessment and adaptive management.
mechanism_description: It implements capacity building for technicians and decision-makers to improve climate vulnerability assessment and adaptive management, strengthening institutional adaptive capacity.
primary_causal_pathway: Capacity building → improved climate risk/vulnerability assessment + decision quality → better adaptation actions → ↓ exposure/sensitivity and/or ↑ coping capacity → ↓ climate damages
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes: Builds skills, procedures, and organizational capability among technicians and decision-makers to conduct climate vulnerability assessments and manage adaptation measures. (2) Mediating process: Improved identification, prioritization, and integration of climate risks into plans/budgets/operations increases institutional adaptive capacity and the effectiveness/timeliness of adaptation decisions. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced vulnerability and impacts (e.g., avoided losses, service disruptions, and health/safety harms) from climate hazards.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced climate risk and damages through better-informed planning and adaptive management enabled by improved vulnerability assessment capacity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Governance capacity building improves risk assessment and adaptive management, leading to reduced exposure/sensitivity and improved coping capacity under climate hazards.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The main intended and largest climate effect is resilience/risk reduction (Adaptation), not emissions reduction (Mitigation), not “same service with less energy/material” (Resource Efficiency), and not ecosystem-mediated benefits (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: While improved capacity could indirectly enable mitigation planning, those effects are secondary and not guaranteed across all instances of this mechanism.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy whose core mechanism is capacity building for vulnerability assessment/adaptive management primarily targets improved adaptive capacity and reduced climate risk, which is squarely Adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the resulting adaptation is focused on floods, heat, drought, critical infrastructure, public health, etc. depends on policy-level evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: May have indirect mitigation co-benefits if the strengthened planning capacity is later used to design emissions-reduction measures, but the dominant and consistent pathway is institutional adaptation capacity and risk reduction.
key_indicators: “Capacity building actions”; “technicians and decision-makers”; “climate vulnerability assessment”; “adaptive management”
co_benefits: Improved general institutional decision-making and risk-management capacity beyond climate (e.g., emergency preparedness, budgeting/process improvements).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as an incentive-program or procurement-requirement if the source specified funding mechanisms or contracted training providers; with current text it is a general capacity-building/planning action.
